data.title: Dream Finders Homes: Land-Light Model Fairly Valued
data.description:
Dream Finders Homes, Inc. (NYSE:DFH) has had a tough year in terms of stock price being down significantly. The company has generated positive net income for the last 5 years and has been in growth mode, with 2019 income at $39 million and the trailing 12-month at $339 million.

Homebuilders usually don't have extreme competitive advantages, but some attributes can be fostered to drive good economic returns. The homebuilder business model structure matters as it can be capital-intensive. As long-term investors, this is exactly what we don't want. DFH has fostered a different route to home building by using a land-light business model.

Avoiding Large Land Investments

A typical homebuilder owns the land, develops the land, and builds on spec or sells before building. This whole scenario makes the builder own a large piece of land, which is a big investment and ties up capital. It can also go really south for the companies that own the land, as the land could decline in value, and now their capital is tied up in an asset that has to be marked down.

DFH has chosen a different path, using an outsourced third party to own and process the land and get it ready to build on. This allows DFH to only purchase the land when it's ready to build on and protects itself from adverse market forces.

The model has really changed the game, allowing hundreds of millions of capital being freed up instead of being stashed in land investments. It's difficult for competitors to change course also, as large investments in land banks already sit on their books, so pivoting to the model takes time.

Management wanted a more nimble/flexible approach while de-risking the balance sheet. The slide below really highlights why management went with this strategy:

The Business Model Works

NVR (NVR) was one of the first to use the land-light model and has proven it works by generating high returns on equity for decades. In addition, the stock price has provided substantial returns to investors.

DFH is copying NVR's strategy, and their numbers prove the strategy is working, as they've consistently produced a 35% plus return on equity over the years.

Alignment with Investors

Patrick Zalupski's is the type of CEO you want running your business. Provides good insights into the business through annual shareholder letters and owns 60% plus of the business. I repeat, he owns 60% plus of the business and is the majority of his net worth. With such a large stake, it's natural for the captain of the ship to think long-term and navigate the business through rough seas. Historically, the leadership of the company has made sound decisions with capital allocation. Capital allocation is such an imperative part of growing a business, especially in the homebuilding space. Patrick's track record so far has been strong, indicating superior management capabilities.

Historically, the company had to lever up substantially in order to make as many acquisitions as they've made. These investments are important for growth as homebuilders need to secure finished lots and penetrate new markets. Once things are running smoothly and cash flow starts to be generated from the investments, debt paydown can occur along with returning capital to shareholders.

Management has done a good job with this cycle.

In 2020 and 2021, the company was highly levered and has been able to reduce its ratios from 167% to 91% in 5 years. EBITDA has also compounded substantially with the acquisitions, making it a more favorable debt/EBITDA ratio.

Valuation

High interest rates and a weaker market have really put strain on the stock prices of builders over the last 12 months. Dream Finders Homes is no exception to this.

At one point, shares were trading as high as $39 a share, and lately, it has been hovering between $20 and $27 a share. Earnings per share are around $3 per share, giving an 11% earnings yield at the current market price. Context is key here, as an 11% yield isn't unusual at times in an industry with such variable dynamics. From a peer analysis, it looks like we get a little discount on the earnings yield against peers.

With home builders, buying at favorable valuations really can protect us from the downsize, as adverse market dynamics do occur in the home building market. Earnings can be variable because of factors out of management's control. Interest rate and household income play a huge factor on the demand side of this industry. As investors, we need to protect ourselves by purchasing shares cheaply, and I'm not seeing that protection with Dream Finders Homes yet. I would like to see the company continue to drive positive earnings and start returning a little more to the buyback program.

Risks

Interest rates and the concentration of where they operate are the two factors I'm looking at closely. Interest rates continue to be elevated making it much more difficult for homebuyers.

There hasn't been much relief from the high rates over the last 6 months, as you can see from the graph above, staying in the high 6% on the 30-year mortgage.

Operation concentration is also a risk, as Dream Finders Homes has benefited over the years with COVID, as people were moving to the southeast and Texas. Only time will tell how they will do in more of a normal market cycle.

Conclusion

Be patient with this company, as I do believe it is one to have on the watchlist. I like the land-light business model, owner operator CEO with massive skin in the game, and already generating some favorable metrics in a very difficult industry. The macroeconomic situation isn't favorable, which is driving my decision to be cautious and wait patiently for a better entry.

data.title: British American Tobacco H1: Stock Price Up, Vuse Disappoints, I Sell Some Shares
data.description:
British American Tobacco p.l.c. (NYSE:BTI, LSE: BATS) is one of the dividend-paying companies with the greatest weight in my portfolio. For years, I have always considered it a buy or strong buy, but after the recent rise and the H1 2025 results, I decided to sell some shares at a profit.

As a result, I believe that today it is more appropriate to rate it as a Hold. Let me explain why in detail.

Highlights H1 2025

Before commenting on the results for H1 2025, I think it is worth highlighting BTI's performance since the beginning of the year as it has been impressive.

A +46% price return that does not take into account the huge dividend, a gain far greater than what you would have earned by investing in the S&P 500. As much as I appreciate BTI, I believe that what has happened is quite anomalous; after all, we are talking about a company with low growth or even negative growth in some cases.

The challenge of increasing cash from operations has always been there given the struggles of the industry it operates in, but in January the stock was so undervalued that even with low-single digit growth there was a good chance of achieving significant returns (which is why I gave it a buy rating).

This was indeed the case, but today we are in a different situation. As we will see, BTI continues to have growth problems, but its valuation multiples are no longer the same as in previous months.

Let's be clear, the multiple is not in a bubble (otherwise I would not have sold just a few shares), but I believe it has expanded in a way that is not in line with fundamentals. I would have justified a higher multiple in light of significantly improving fundamentals, but that is not what I saw in H1 2025.

Compared to H1 2024, revenues in £ fell by 2.20%, but that's not what worries me so much. We all know that the tobacco industry is bound to decline gradually over time, and the goal of these companies is to steer consumers toward less harmful alternatives. Yet revenue from new categories has remained flat. Frankly, I expected better results, especially since competitors such as Philip Morris (PM) are already much less dependent on the sale of combustible tobacco products.

Philip Morris' smoke-free products are growing at double digits despite accounting for almost a third of total sales. According to BTI, they account for only 18.20% of sales, and compared to H1 2024, there has been an improvement of just 70 basis points. In my opinion, the company is taking too long to transition from combustible products to smoke-free products.

Total smokeless products (New Categories + Traditional Oral) in the US saw sales decline by 0.80%: we are off track if the goal is to replace cigarettes. Of course, Modern Oral grew at an impressive rate of 372% following the launch of Velo Plus, but this segment represents only 10% of the Total Smokeless segment.

Vapor, through the Vuse brand, is by far the most important market for BTI, accounting for 41% of the Total Smokeless segment, and has seen sales decline by as much as 13.80%. In effect, it has wiped out the progress made in Modern Oral.

The company continues to blame the proliferation of illicit disposable vapes in the US, the world's largest vapor market. According to BTI estimates, over 50% of it is based on illicit vapes, and solving this problem would give a huge boost to its sales.

However, the months are passing and I see no improvement in this regard. Let me be clear, this is not BTI's fault, but we cannot assume that this problem will suddenly disappear. The company has several new products in the pipeline to attract new customers, including Vuse Ultra, and I hope it succeeds, although I am slightly pessimistic about this.

Our new premium innovation, Vuse Ultra, offers consumers a highly differentiated, connected and customisable experience. We are encouraged by the early performance in Canada and will continue the roll-out in a targeted way through H2 2025.

All in all, compared to a company like Philip Morris, I believe that BTI is lagging far behind in terms of product innovation. The results mentioned above are those obtained in the US, but the situation is no better in other geographical areas.

BTI is still too dependent on combustible tobacco, and this factor, combined with the rapid increase in the share price, led me to sell some shares at a significant profit.

The reason I did not sell my entire position is that I still believe in BTI's potential for double-digit growth in smokeless products, and management is more optimistic about the second half of the year. In addition, the company is experiencing significant improvements in debt sustainability, an issue that scared investors until a few years ago.

Net debt, although still high, fell by 10% compared to last year and there are no significant debt maturities in the next 12 months. From this point of view, the gradual sale of the stake in ITC is helping a lot.

These inflows of billions of pounds are making BTI's financial structure much stronger, and I expect this trend to continue. Consider that in H1, proceeds from the sale of ITC shares were £904 million, and BTI's ownership fell by 2.50%. However, as of June 30, 2025, it still stands at 22.93%, so there is still plenty of room to unlock further billions.

The knowledge that it can quickly tap into such large sums is probably why BTI increased its 2025 share repurchase by £200 million to £1.10 billion. Shareholders always like buybacks, but I invite you to consider an alternative point of view.

Buybacks are most effective when the share price is low, so it would have made much more sense to increase them when the stock was falling. Doing buybacks at the wrong time when your debt is still quite high can backfire. Management probably thinks that BTI shares are still undervalued, which would justify the buyback, but we'll see. Personally, I would have preferred that this £1.10 billion be used to invest in smokeless products or further reduce debt, but that is just my opinion.

Finally, in addition to the stake in ITC, the other reason that leads me to hold BTI is the sustainability of its dividend despite its very high; the cash dividend payout ratio is only 54%. Those who buy it now get 6%, while those who bought it last year may have achieved a double-digit dividend on cost.

Conclusion

Overall, BTI remains a solid company with a significant stake in the ITC giant and increasingly sustainable debt. Dividends and buybacks make it a highly attractive company for those seeking a passive income that is not too risky.

The tobacco industry is in decline, as we all know, but it still has a long way to go before it ceases to exist. Consider that BTI's revenues from combustible products in the US recorded sales growth of 1.10% in H1 2025, so there are still years, if not decades, to exploit this market.

My only concern stems from the slow pace of improvement in the smokeless segment, which should be growing the most in this transition phase (Philip Morris is well ahead). Vuse is disappointing, and even though modern oral is growing rapidly, it has not managed to make the overall result less bitter. This factor, combined with the rapid growth in the share price in recent months, has led me to sell some shares and remain vigilant about Vuse's growth in H2. Management has promised a recovery, so we'll see.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: lululemon: A Quality Compounder Trading At Discounted Multiples
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I believe lululemon athletica inc. (NASDAQ:LULU) has the hallmarks of a typical quality company. It has ample reinvestment opportunities, a high-level ROIC, a healthy balance sheet, an ability to generate high free cash flows and a favorable shareholder return policy. However, over the past period, the company's fundamentals have strengthened, but its market valuation remains near its lowest levels. I believe this is a good chance for public investors who want to invest in a quality stock at a discounted price.

In this article, I talk about lululemon's recent results and why it is a great quality company. And I estimated the expected return if the market fairly re-evaluates its value.

Company Snapshot

lululemon is a premium athletic clothing company best known for yoga and fitness wear. In 2024, it reported $10.6 billion in revenue, up 10% from the prior year. Growth has slowed compared to past years, but over the last five years, the company has posted a significant growth rate of 22%, which is much faster than its peers.

lululemon runs its business in three main regions: the Americas, Mainland China, and the Rest of the World. In 2024, the Americas made up 75% of total revenue, growing only 4% from last year. This slower growth made some investors worry about the U.S. market. Mainland China brought in 13% of revenue and grew 41%, becoming the company’s main growth driver. The Rest of World segment had 12% of revenue and grew 27%.

From its latest earnings call, lululemon kept its full-year sales growth target at 7–8%. The company said it may also raise prices to handle tariffs, which are expected to hurt most in Q2. I think the impact will be short-term. With a gross margin of nearly 60%, the company shows it has strong pricing power and a brand that customers value.

Why It Is a Great Quality Company

The typical logic behind quality investing is to invest in companies that are financially sound and have a long-term competitive advantage, thereby pursuing long-term compounding effects. For this to be realized, a company's business must generate a return higher than its cost of capital and there must be ample opportunities to reinvest that business.

Typically, a company's competitive advantage can be quantitatively measured through its ROIC calculation. lululemon has demonstrated an excellent and sustainably high ROIC of above 40%.

[ROIC Calculation, Unit: $ Millions]

Jan-21

Jan-22

Jan-23

Jan-24

Feb-25
EBIT 813 1,375 1,726 2,211 2,506
Tax expenses 230 359 478 626 762
Operating profits after tax 582 1,016 1,248 1,585 1,744
Cash 1,151 1,260 1,155 2,244 1,984
Total Debt 799 881 1,070 1,403 1,576
Total Equity 2,559 2,740 3,149 4,232 4,324

Invested capital

2,207

2,361

3,064

3,391

3,916
ROIC 44.5% 46.0% 49.1% 47.7%

lululemon's ability to consistently achieve such high ROIC is due to its strong brand and pricing power. Brand is important. The stronger the brand, the greater the customer loyalty, leading to repeat purchases and greater pricing power.

lululemon's key differentiating strategy is community-based marketing. Unlike traditional sports brands, which often employ famous athletes or celebrities, lululemon appoints local yoga instructors, Pilates instructors, and running coaches as ambassadors. They hold classes in lululemon's stores, naturally fostering an emotional connection between the brand and consumers. lululemon shifted the in-store experience from a simple purchase to an education-focused experience. Limited price discounts and promotions help stimulate a value-driven consumer mentality.

Thanks to its differentiation strategy, lululemon maintains a gross profit margin in the high 50% range, which proves it enjoys a price premium of about 20% compared to the sector median of 38%. And, the inventory level is consistently maintained at around 15% of sales, which appears to maintain high levels of customer loyalty and repeat purchases.

[Gross profit margins and Inventory levels Calculation, Unit: $ Millions]

Jan-21

Jan-22

Jan-23

Jan-24

Feb-25
Revenue 4,402 6,257 8,111 9,619 10,588
Cost of goods sold 1,938 2,648 3,618 4,010 4,317

Gross Profit

 2,464 3,609 4,492 5,609 6,271

Gross Profit Margins

56%

58%

55%

58%

 59%

Inventory

 647 1,009 1,447 1,324 1,442
% Inventory 15% 16% 18% 14% 14%

Next, let's examine the company's growth potential. As mentioned earlier, the company's current largest market is the Americas, where approximately 75% of its sales are generated. In 2024, the Americas segment reported approximately $790 million in sales, a mere 3.9% year-over-year growth. Despite the slowdown in the US market, considering the overall sales growth of 10%, I believe the company has ample reinvestment opportunities in markets outside the US.

China, in particular, presents a wealth of growth opportunities. Sales in China reached approximately $1,361 million in 2024, a 41.3% year-on-year increase. The number of stores in China reached 151, representing 20% of the company's 767 total stores and an 18.9% increase from 127 the previous year, demonstrating the strongest growth among all regions. This increase in store count will likely serve as a foundation for future profit growth and contribute to sustained growth.

Even compared to the great sports brand Nike. In 2024, Nike's sales in China reported $7,545 million, a 4% year-over-year increase. This figure is approximately 5.5 times larger than lululemon's sales in China. Assuming lululemon continues to grow at a 20% annual rate in the Chinese market, this suggests reinvestment opportunities for the next nine to ten years.

In short, economic moats such as a strong brand and long-term growth opportunities provide the perfect combination for investors seeking compounding benefits. Let's also touch on a few other quality factors.

A stable financial structure is important to maximize long-term compounding effects because a highly leveraged financial structure makes it difficult for a company to withstand unexpected economic cycles. In 2024, lululemon has approximately $1,500 million in debt and $1,900 million in cash, meaning it has negative net debt.

And lululemon's CAPEX over the past five years is approximately $500 million, and its leverage calculated by excluding CAPEX from EBITDA is also less than 1x, so lululemon's financial leverage is very low and it possesses high financial flexibility.

[Leverage ratio, Unit: $ Millions]

Total Debt 799 881 1,070 1,403 1,576

EBITDA

 998 1,599 2,018 2,590 2,952

Leverage (x)

0.8

0.6

0.5

0.5

0.5
Capex (229) (395) (639) (652) (689)

EBITDAX

769

1,205

1,379

1,938

2,263

Leverage (x)

1.04

0.73

0.78

0.72

0.70

Finally, no matter how much money a company earns, if it fails to use it to increase shareholder value, it will inevitably be undervalued by the market. The most common ways a company returns value to shareholders are dividends and share repurchases. lululemon primarily returns value to shareholders through share repurchases.

Over the past five years, lululemon has returned approximately $740 million to shareholders in the form of stock repurchases. The amount of stock repurchases continues to increase. The size of share repurchases, which was only 12% of operating cash flow five years ago, increased to 73% in 2024, and on average over the five years, approximately 45% of the money earned from operations was returned to shareholders. Considering that capex investment is approximately 36% of operating cash flow, there is room to increase share repurchases by approximately 20% going forward.

Valuation

Quality investing focuses on maximizing compounding. To capitalize on this benefit, long-term investments are preferred over short-term ones. Therefore, for valuation purposes, I estimated future cash flows for at least five years and calculated the expected investment return.

First, I assumed different sales growth rates for each market due to market opportunities. With annual growth of 4% in the US, 20% in China, and 15% in other markets, a company-wide sales growth rates are expected to be approximately 8% for the next five years.

[Revenue Forecasts, Unit: $ Millions]

Feb-25 FY1 FY2 FY3 FY4 FY5
Americas 7,928 8,245 8,575 8,918 9,275 9,646
China Mainland 1,361 1,634 1,960 2,352 2,823 3,387
Rest of World 1,299 1,493 1,717 1,975 2,271 2,612
Total Revenue 10,588 11,372 12,253 13,246 14,369 15,645

Next, I forecasted its profitability for the next five years. Considering that lululemon's competitive advantage, brand power, has been maintained stably over the past period, I believe that its economic moat will remain strong and will be able to maintain the company's profitability in the future. Therefore, I assume its gross profit margin and operating margin will remain at their five-year average going forward.

[Margin Forecasts, Unit: $ Millions]

Feb-25 FY1 FY2 FY3 FY4 FY5
Total Revenue 10,588 11,372 12,253 13,246 14,369 15,645
Gross Profit 6,271 6,518 7,023 7,592 8,236 8,967
Gross Profit Margins 59% 57% 57% 57% 57% 57%
SG&A 3,762 4,043 4,356 4,709 5,109 5,563
% of Revenue 36% 36% 36% 36% 36% 36%
EBIT 2,508 2,475 2,666 2,883 3,127 3,405
EBIT Margins 24% 22% 22% 22% 22% 22%
Depreciation 444 435 468 506 549 598
% of Revenue 4% 4% 4% 4% 4% 4%
EBITDA 2,952 2,910 3,135 3,389 3,676 4,003
EBITDA Margins 28% 26% 26% 26% 26% 26%

The last is to forecast free cash flows. FCF is calculated by subtracting capex, working capital investments, and taxes from EBITDA. And then, I assume that 50% of FCF is used for share repurchases. To calculate FCF before share repurchase, I used the 5-year average of each item. And I assume that 50% of the FCF calculated this way is used for share repurchases, with the remaining 50% retained as a cash balance.

[Cash Flow Projection, Unit: $ Millions]

Feb-25 FY1 FY2 FY3 FY4 FY5
EBITDA 3,396 3,344 3,603 3,895 4,225 4,601
CAPEX (689) (743) (801) (866) (939) (1022)
Change in WC (212) (276) (297) (321) (348) (379)
Cash Income Tax paid (579) (687) (740) (800) (868) (945)
FCF before buybacks 1916 1638 1765 1908 2070 2254
Share Repurchase (1672) (819) (883) (954) (1035) (1127)
FCF after buybacks 244 819 883 954 1035 1127
Cash Balance 1,984 2,803 3,686 4,640 5,675 6,802

The company's current market valuation is around 8–9x EV/EBITDA. The sector median is 10x, and lululemon's five-year average trading multiple was 26x. In other words, lululemon is trading at a significant undervaluation compared to its peer group and historical levels.

However, I view lululemon not simply as a retailer, but as a quality company. I frequently compare it to the Dow Jones Industrial Average's 30 non-financial companies as a comparable benchmark for quality companies. These companies have long operating records across various industries and hold leading positions in their industries, making them worthy of comparison as quality companies.

These companies are currently trading at an average EV/EBITDA multiple of approximately 16x. Given their average sales growth rate of 2.8%, EBITDA margin of 25%, and return on capital of 11.1%, I see no reason for lululemon to be valued any lower than these companies. Therefore, I believe lululemon deserves a market valuation of up to 15x over the long term.

[List of Comparable Quality Companies]

Symbol Name EV / EBITDA Revenue YoY EBITDA Margin Return on Total Capital
AAPL Apple Inc. 22.47 4.91% 34.68% 46.31%
AMGN Amgen Inc. 13.47 15.56% 46.08% 9.67%
BA The Boeing Company NM 2.41% -8.88% -11.61%
CAT Caterpillar Inc. 16.10 -5.58% 23.38% 14.14%
CRM Salesforce, Inc. 22.33 7.97% 29.04% 6.80%
CSCO Cisco Systems, Inc. 19.23 0.47% 26.50% 9.67%
CVX Chevron Corporation 9.02 1.19% 19.73% 7.46%
DIS The Walt Disney Company 13.44 5.42% 20.33% 5.81%
DOW Dow Inc. 8.95 -2.82% 9.24% 1.70%
HD The Home Depot, Inc. 17.10 7.33% 15.65% 21.80%
HON Honeywell International Inc. 16.90 7.11% 24.90% 10.47%
IBM International Business Machines Corporation 20.93 2.69% 22.15% 7.58%
INTC Intel Corporation 13.73 -3.72% 17.34% -1.03%
JNJ Johnson & Johnson 14.44 4.68% 33.38% 11.76%
KO The Coca-Cola Company 21.30 1.28% 33.53% 12.01%
MCD McDonald's Corporation 19.28 -0.23% 54.06% 15.44%
MMM 3M Company 15.61 0.24% 23.55% 16.59%
MRK Merck & Co., Inc. 8.00 1.82% 45.83% 18.81%
MSFT Microsoft Corporation 25.71 14.13% 55.25% 19.42%
NKE NIKE, Inc. 26.26 -9.84% 9.67% 9.14%
PG The Procter & Gamble Company 16.83 0.29% 27.64% 14.94%
VZ Verizon Communications Inc. 7.08 2.05% 36.34% 7.12%
WBA Walgreens Boots Alliance, Inc. 13.00 6.22% 1.96% 1.46%
WMT Walmart Inc. 19.90 4.22% 6.24% 11.75%
AVERAGE 16.57 2.8% 25.3% 11.1%

Applying a multiple of 15x to lululemon's five-year projected EBITDA yields an EV of approximately $60 billion. To get the expected equity value, I assume the debt remains at the current level and the cash balance would accumulate with annual free cash flows. Then, the equity value is expected to be around $65 billion. With a current market cap of $25 billion, this represents approximately 2.5x growth and an IRR of approximately 20%.

In short, buying a good business at a low multiple and waiting for the market to revalue it is a solid investment idea.

Conclusion

lululemon has many traits of a quality company. It has a strong brand, healthy cash flow, low debt, and a favorable shareholder return policy. However, sometimes the market misses good investment opportunities or overreacts to temporary factors, resulting in good stocks being undervalued.

I believe lululemon is a strong company that the market is ignoring. Over time, I think it will get the revaluation it deserves and give a huge return to investors who move proactively.

data.title: Honeywell: Price Pullback Is A Great Opportunity
data.description:
Hunting for stock bargains can be a highly rewarding endeavor. However, looking only at stocks with low PE ratios can cause one to miss out on some great long-term wealth compounders. These are what I would call 'set it and forget it' types of stocks that ought to be accumulated when they are reasonably priced. That's because these companies have moat-worthy businesses that rarely, if ever, result in a 'cheap' valuation. Bottom of Form

Such I find the case to be with Honeywell International (NASDAQ:HON), which I last covered back in April, highlighting an opportune time to buy the stock, its focus on energy security and digitalization, and long-term growth prospects in aerospace and automation. That turned out to be good timing, as HON has delivered a 19% total return for investors since my last piece.

The total return would have been higher had it not been for the recent dip in price from $240 to $222. While HON isn't cheap, it's far from being expensive, either, with a forward PE of 21. In this article, I revisit HON, including recent business fundamentals, and discuss why the recent pullback in price presents a solid entry point for this GARP (growth at a reasonable price) stock, so let's get started!

Why HON?

Honeywell International is a diversified industrial conglomerate. It operates in four key segments of Aerospace Technologies (commercial, defense, and space), Industrial Automation (process solutions, warehouse automations, sensing technologies), Building Automation (HVAC, energy management), and Energy & Sustainability (advanced materials, sustainable fuels).

Notably, management plans to unlock shareholder value, similar to what industrial peer GE (GE) did, by spinning off its Advanced Materials and Aerospace segments in two separate transactions over the next 12 months. This creates 3 distinct entities and enables RemainCo to focus on its Automation core competency across the Industrial and Building sectors.

This could help HON to create shareholder value, as the three standalone businesses could be worth more than the sum of the parts. As shown below, HON had a decent track record of beating the S&P 500 (SPY) in total return over the past 10 years until 2023, when it started to lag the overall market.

Honeywell demonstrated respectable results during Q2 2025, with organic sales rising by 5% YoY, which was above the high end of guidance range. This was led by robust growth in Building Automation, which saw its third consecutive quarter of 8% sales growth. As shown below, all segments except for Industrial Automation saw growth in the 6–8% range during the second quarter.

More encouraging is HON's higher profit growth (compared to revenue growth) of 8% YoY, due to segment margin expansion in the faster growing Building and Industrial Automation segments. This resulted in robust adjusted EPS growth of 10% YoY, exceeding the upper end of management's guidance by 5 cents. Notably, HON increased R&D spend in all business segments, with it rising to 4.6% of sales. This upfront investment is designed to accelerate innovation-led growth in future periods.

Management raised its full-year revenue growth guidance from 3% previously to 4.5% at the midpoint of range and adjusted EPS growth of 7% to $10.55 at the midpoint of range. This is driven by management's expectations for segment margin growth of 50 basis points over the prior year. Despite higher tariffs and ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties, HON expects pricing productivity and alternative sourcing strategies to mitigate those impacts.

Near-term growth is supported by bolt-on acquisitions like the $1.8 billion purchase of Johnson Matthey's Catalyst Technologies business to enhance the UOP segment (energy solutions business). This transaction gives HON additional capabilities in sustainable methanol, sustainable aviation fuel, hydrogen, and ammonia to serve its customer base.

Another acquisition is Li-ion Tamer, which enhances HON's building automation capabilities in the high-growth energy and data center markets. Building automation has a sizable $100+ billion addressable market globally, and that's expected to grow at a robust 8.8% CAGR between now and the end of this decade. Additionally, the Industrial Automation market is expected to grow at a 9% CAGR through 2032 to $493 billion, as shown below.

HON is supported by a strong balance sheet with an 'A' credit rating from S&P. It has a very high cash and short-term investment balance of $10.7 billion and a safe net debt-to-TTM EBITDA ratio of 2.7x. The stock currently yields 2%, and the dividend is well covered by a 43% payout ratio. It also comes with a five-year dividend CAGR of 4.9% and 5 consecutive years of growth.

Admittedly, HON's yield isn't high, but it also returns capital to shareholders through stock buybacks, making it a total return play. As shown below, HON has reduced nearly 10% of its outstanding float over the past 5 years.

Lastly, while HON isn't cheap, it's far from being expensive, either, at the current price of $222 with a forward PE of 21.0. This is considering potential value creation opportunities from the planned spinoff of the Advanced Materials business later this year and the Aerospace spin-off planned for the second half of next year.

If the success of the GE spin-offs is of any indication, investors could see meaningful gains given the unique growth trajectories of each business. In particular, HON's Aerospace division provides technologies across a spectrum of commercial, business jets, and defense applications, making it a very well-rounded business. Based on my expectations for ~10% EPS growth annually on a combined basis due to the aforementioned catalysts, the dividend, and share buybacks, HON could produce potential double-digit total returns from here.

HON could see cost inflation from tariffs. While management appears to have a cost mitigation plan in place to counter tariffs, uncertainties in long-term tariff rates could result in margin compression. Recession risks in Europe and China could hurt short-cycle businesses like Sensing and Thermal Solutions. Lastly, spin-offs and potential for changes in valuations of these businesses are near-term risks.

Investor Takeaway

Honeywell is a solid opportunity for long-term investors seeking growth at a reasonable price. The stock remains attractively valued, especially after the recent pullback, given its strong fundamentals and strategic plans to unlock shareholder value through upcoming spin-offs of its Aerospace and Advanced Materials businesses.

HON's robust Q2 performance, increased R&D investment, and bolt-on acquisitions in high-growth sectors like sustainable fuels and building automation position it for continued earnings momentum. Honeywell is also backed by a solid balance sheet, disciplined capital returns, and exposure to secular growth markets. As such, HON presents a GARP opportunity with the potential for strong total returns over the coming years.

data.title: Unlocking The Future Of Energy Transition
data.description:
Transcript

Grover Burthey: We are pleased to be joined today by David Crane. David is the newly announced chairman of Generate Capital, the former undersecretary for infrastructure at the US Department of Energy, and also the former CEO of US Utility, NRG.

David Crane: Thanks for having me. It's great to see you and great to be at PIMCO again.

Grover Burthey: So, of course, most of your experience has been here in the US, but what do you think is interesting outside of the US from an energy perspective?

David Crane: I mean, particularly with PIMCO and the fact that you do things at scale, large scale, I mean, there's certain things that we were all excited about 10 years ago, electrification in Africa and things, which is really important.

But yeah, I've probably looked at 50 companies that have been trying to do it over the last, and no one's just figured out scale. So what I do for someone that's looking in the electricity space and wants to invest big amounts of money is say, well, when it comes to electricity, it comes, there's only about 15 countries that are big enough to really matter.

There's countries like Brazil, but it's really hard to invest in electricity in a country that's largely dependent on hydro, because if you're investing in hydro and you have a dry season, then you're hurt. If you're investing in something to compete against hydro, and there's a wet season, you're hurt. So it's not that predictable.

Grover Burthey: But within this more nascent low-carbon space, anything that you find particularly interesting or compelling?

David Crane: I think geothermal's worth looking at. I also think because electricity at the very least is going to become probably doubly as expensive as it's been, that will give rise to software and other integrated solutions that just get back in the sort of boring area of what's been perceived as boring area of energy efficiency. You know, because the electricity system in the United States works at 42% capacity or something.

And I think there's going to be a lot of solutions that just people will trend towards becoming smarter about their electricity consumption when they realize that their monthly bill for electricity is not going to be $150 a month anymore. People that are offering solutions like that, I think, are going to be in a very good place.

Grover Burthey: What do you see as potential mitigating factors to actually be able to meet that expected power demand, particularly in a world where we have potential supply chain disruption and different macroeconomic uncertainty?

David Crane: Well, on top of being green, solar and wind, those are the two forms of large-scale generation capacity in the United States that can add incremental capacity faster than any other.

If you need an extra gigawatt of electricity and you looked at the technologies, how long would it take solar to ramp up a gig? One year. Wind? Maybe two years. Gas-fired combined cycle? That's going to depend on GE building another manufacturing plant because they're sold out for the next seven years. So I'd say five to six years for gas. Coal, I'd just say never, because I just don't think anyone's going to build a coal plant, and nuclear, 2035 at the earliest.

Grover Burthey: Okay. So you combine all that, where are there financing gaps? Where is the market perhaps overlooking investment opportunities?

David Crane: A fair number of the technologies and distributed or in clean technologies are pretty highly distributed, sort of five to a hundred million dollar total project costs, investments.

And there's a financing gap there because you have to solve that age-old equation that the work that you would need to do to invest in that five to a hundred million dollar asset is almost exactly the same amount of work that you would need to do to invest in something that was $500 million to a billion. And you have to put a lot of money to work, and other people have to put a lot of money to work. So that sector, I think, has historically been a little bit underfinanced, underbanked. And again, with it being largely staying local, it's an area that I think is going to be very attractive.

Disclosure

All investments contain risk and may lose value.

data.title: Stellantis: Valuation Makes No Sense
data.description:
Stellantis (NYSE: NYSE:STLA) is one of those stocks that everyone seems to hate right now, and that’s exactly why I like it.

Let’s get the obvious out of the way. Yes, the company just posted a rough quarter. Yes, tariffs are weighing on results. And yes, they’ve got some heavy lifting to do in North America and Europe. But here’s the thing: none of that justifies a stock trading at a 68% discount to book value, let alone 4x earnings.

That’s where the opportunity is. This is a €23B global automaker with iconic brands like Jeep, Ram, Dodge, Peugeot, and Alfa Romeo. It’s not overleveraged. And yet the market is treating it like a dying business. That disconnect is where deep value lives.

Way Under Book, Under 4x Earnings

Right now, STLA trades around $9. Its book value per share? Close to $28. That’s a 68% discount to book. This is a manufacturing powerhouse with one of the strongest balance sheets in the industry, sitting on over €31B in cash and equivalents.

Meanwhile, Toyota (TM) trades at over 1x book. General Motors (GM) is at 0.75x. Even Ford (F), which has its own problems, is at book. STLA is the only one this deep in value territory.

And look at earnings: the company trades at 4.7x 2025 earnings, 3.5x forward. You don’t see those kinds of numbers unless something’s seriously wrong… or seriously misunderstood.

Yes, 1H25 Numbers Were Ugly

In the first half of 2025, shipments dropped 7%, revenue fell 13%, and operating income took a hit. Adjusted operating income (AOI) came in at just €540M, and cash flow was negative €3B.

But here’s what most investors missed: things are already turning around. Management was clear about it on the call. H1 was bad, but it was still an improvement over the second half of 2024. Volumes are recovering, pricing is improving, and a new wave of products is about to hit.

CFO Doug Ostermann said it straight:

Consolidated shipments of 2.7 million units fell 7% with declines in North America and in large Europe, slightly mitigated by growth in South America and Middle East and Africa. Net revenue of EUR 74 billion saw a larger 13% decline as adverse regional mix and lower pricing added to the change in volumes. AOI margins were compressed as the early stage of our recovery actions was negatively impacted by external factors, like tariffs and foreign exchange headwinds. Our adjusted diluted earnings per share generally track our AOI development, and our industrial free cash flow was an outflow of EUR 3 billion in the first half. While nowhere near our potential, each of these 5 figures did, in fact, improve in H1 2025 compared to the second half of 2024 as we benefited from new products, improved inventory discipline and more stable production schedules compared to the prior 6 months.

So, while YoY looks rough, the sequential trend is what actually matters, and it’s headed the right way.

Tariffs Are Real, But So Is Overreaction

Let’s talk tariffs, since that’s the headline everyone’s freaking out about. STLA expects to take a €1.5B hit this year, mostly in the back half. That’s a big number, no doubt. But the stock already got hammered for it, down more than 31% so far this year.

Here’s what people are missing. STLA is actually in a stronger position than most of its peers. About 95% of what they sell in the US is made in Mexico or Canada, and it all falls under the USMCA rules. That means they avoid the worst of the new tariffs that are hitting companies like Volkswagen.

More than half of their AOI comes from parts of the world that are not even impacted by these US tariffs. And they are not standing still. STLA has been shifting production, tweaking component sourcing, and taking advantage of the new 3.75% MSRP credit for vehicles made in the US.

So yes, €1.5B is a hit. But it is not the disaster people think. STLA saw this coming and already adjusted.

Big Brands Are Coming Back

One of the reasons things fell apart last year is that STLA had a bunch of major product gaps. Models like the Jeep Cherokee, Dodge Charger, and Ram DS were all pulled from the market at once, creating a big revenue hole.

Now they’re filling that gap. The Jeep Cherokee is back in H2 with a new HEV powertrain. The HEMI V8 Ram is making a comeback this fall, with 10,000 orders in the first 24 hours. And the new Dodge Charger hits production later this year.

CEO Antonio Filosa admitted they made some bad calls in the past:

For instance, in North America, U.S. specifically, we phased out 7 successful nameplates: Jeep Cherokee, Jeep Renegade, Chrysler 300, Ram DS Classic, Ram ProMaster CD, Dodge Charger, the muscle car of the past, Dodge Challenger, same family. Those 7 nameplates were granting to us 300,000 units per year sold and several billions of gross commercial result per year. So now we are restoring that lineup.

Now they’re reversing that and reconnecting with core customers.

A Cash Cow Waiting to Reboot

Even in this “ugly” half, STLA still brought in €540M in AOI. And they’re sitting on €47B in industrial liquidity. This isn’t a liquidity crisis. It’s a temporary margin reset, and one that looks fixable.

H2 should look a lot better. STLA is guiding for positive cash flow, better margins, and more volume. Tariffs go up, but they’re counting on new, higher-margin models to balance it out.

Also worth pointing out: the dividend is fat: currently yielding +8%. And it’s covered. So you’re getting paid to wait.

What Could Go Wrong?

Let’s be clear: just because the stock is dirt cheap doesn’t mean it’s risk-free. A few things could still go the other way.

Tariffs are already hitting hard (€1.5B this year), and that’s if things don’t get worse. If tensions with Mexico or the EU escalate, it could keep dragging into 2026. STLA can’t control that.

STLA is betting big on new launches like the Cherokee and Charger to turn things around. If those flop, the comeback stalls. They’ve already fumbled once, they can’t blow it again.

Europe’s still a weak spot. Demand’s soft, pricing is cutthroat, and margins are thin. If that doesn’t improve, it could cancel out a lot of the progress they’re making in North America.

None of these risks are being ignored by the market, I believe they’re already priced in. But it’s worth keeping in mind that this is a turnaround story, not a victory lap.

My Take

The stock is cheap. Like, this-makes-no-sense cheap.

Under 4x earnings. Under book. World-class brands. And signs of a turnaround already underway. Yes, there are real risks. Tariffs hurt. But all of that is already in the price.

The upside here is a valuation reset. Once the market realizes this isn’t some busted automaker but a temporarily out-of-favor one, the multiple can easily move from 4x to 6x. That alone would put the stock closer to $14.

I’m long, and I’m patient. This one’s a textbook contrarian play.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: INDY: Betting On India's Large Caps
data.description:
Emerging markets are an interesting source of growth, and India is one of them, with an economy projected to grow at 6.4% CAGR in real terms. Of course, this comes with a high volatility in domestic markets, but there is an opportunity.

The iShares India 50 ETF (NASDAQ:INDY) is a bet on India’s best companies, with a clear focus on financials. The fund’s focused portfolio of 50 companies and high top 10 concentration increases its focus, trying to offset the emerging market risk by putting higher weights in large companies such as HDFC Bank (HDB) and Reliance Industries (RELI)

India benefits from tailwinds to monitor, because investing in India means believing in these trends: we are talking about an increasing demand, an accelerating digital transformation, and monetary policies designed to push the economy in international settings.

A Focused Strategy With Strong Returns

The ETF’s portfolio, made of only 50 holdings, is aimed at capturing only the largest companies of the economy, companies that already are large and with a proven track record. Other ETFs investing in India like iShares MSCI India ETF (INDA), another BlackRock fund, have a broader exposure, with 158 holdings, thus a different strategy.

Financials, the biggest industry of the fund with 38% of the fund and key holdings such as HDFC Bank and ICICI Bank (IBN), both accounting for almost a quarter of the fund. Financials are a central industry in India given the country is developing fast, benefiting both from a growing demand and a growing penetration.

The Mutual Fund’s AUM in India increased 6x in the last ten years, and has only an estimated 5% penetration now. Compared with INDA, and its 30% concentration in the financial sector, we can see that INDY is not only more concentrated on top companies but also on top industries.

Smaller industries but still significant are consumer discretionary at 11%, with companies like Tata Motors a bet on the rising EV industry in India and IT at 11% with companies like Infosys (INFY) leading the $80 Bn Indian IT services market, projected to grow at a 13% CAGR. INFY is also well positioned to leverage the surge of A.I. in India, a market projected to reach $17 Bn by 2027, being one of the fastest growing A.I. market of the world.

But, looking at first financial metrics, INDY doesn't appear to be the most attractive one: its expense ratio of 0.89% is high, even for a sector-specific ETF, and more than 20 bps above INDA, standing at 0.62%. INDY’s AUM is also almost 15x times lower, at $697 Mn. The daily trading volume is also low considering the fund’s size, at $5 Mn daily. This is enough for retail investors to trade with, but considering the fund’s size, it will not be enough to cover the biggest trades.

Looking at the performance, you will see below that in the long term (10Y), both performances are almost identical, but with volatility: INDY has performed better in the short term history (1Y), and INDA has performed better in the medium term (3Y to 5Y). I say performed better, but in both time horizons, the performance actually is close and showing mid range double digits growth since COVID.

Indian Tailwinds and INDY’s Positioning

One thing to like about India is that the population is young, increasingly urban and the government is showing strong support through reforms in taxation and infrastructure, core reasons behind the projected 6.4% real growth in GDP.

The portfolio of INDY is targeting fast growing industries within the Indian economy.

For example, the energy industry (10% of the fund), with Reliance Industries as a key holding, is benefiting from the push for energy security by the government. As per the IEA, the energy industry is booming in India, investments representing 3.6% of the GDP with 83% directed to clean energy.

Consumer staples (7% of the fund), with companies like ITC LTD or Hindustan Unilever are leveraging the structural increase in discretionary spending, as the Indian middle class is expected to represent 56% of the country in 5 years.

Now the hot topic, financials. India’s Monetary policy is a key element: the country’s 5.5% repo rate, which recently was 6.5%, is designed to balance inflation with growth to increase the country's stability. I think bank holdings like HDFC will surge as credit growth increases, and as non performing loans are decreasing.

Despite this higher concentration to high growth industries, the P/E ratio of the fund at 22.71 is lower than that of INDA, at 24.72. I like this fact as it shows that the high concentration of the fund doesn't come with a large cap premium.

Risks

As you expect from an emerging markets ETF, the risk level is high.

I like the concentration of INDY’s portfolio because I’m most confident about large cap holdings in emerging markets, but this also comes with additional risks. The 57.86% allocation in the top 10, or even the significant allocation to the top 3 companies (see below) expose the fund to company specific risks.

As the Indian economy is not as resilient as the one of developed markets, macroeconomic risks are high. The country’s reliance on imported oil exposes it to the current tense geopolitical context and could impact the energy holdings of the fund.

I mentioned liquidity before, and given the size of the fund I do think this is a risk. The $5 Mn daily trading volume is small and could imply a strong volatility over buy or sell transactions by major investors.

Conclusion

The concentrated positioning of INDY, focused on major industries like financials and benefiting from structural growth (large young population accessing middle class).

This is a buy for investors wanting exposure for emerging markets, as I see India as the most resilient one.

The fund is aligned with country specific drivers such as banking reforms, digital transformation and consumer growth ; all of it coming with a reasonable P/E ratio and a competitive historical performance.

Risks are high, coming from concentration, geopolitics and the emerging nature of the economy, but I believe it is worth the potential return.
